meta.title: AI: Strategy, Ethics & the Age of Generative Intelligence

slides.headline: AI: Strategy, Ethics & the Age of Generative Intelligence
slides.note: Understanding AI as both a transformative force and an ethical imperative

slides.headline: What Is Generative AI?
slides.subheadline: A natural evolution — not a surprise
bullets: AI: technologies that perform tasks once requiring human minds
Transformer models sit at the core of Gen AI's capabilities
ChatGPT reached 50% US adoption faster than any prior technology
Chatbots outpaced smartphones, TV, radio, and the internet in speed of adoption
Driven by three compounding forces: better models, cheaper GPUs, more data
181 zetabytes of global data generated by 2025 — up from 2 in 2010
slides.note: Gen AI is an expected evolution in computing, not a surprise revolution

slides.headline: Two Ways AI Augments Computing

left.title: Inputs — Abstraction
bullets: Natural language replaces formal code & commands
Anyone can now access specialised software
Interface democratised across skill levels
Evolution: Natural Language → Graphics → Formal Text → Mathematics — reversed

right.title: Outputs — Computation
bullets: Expanding what software can produce
Generation of text, code, images, decisions
AI agents act autonomously on explicit data
More people using more computers for more tasks

slides.note: The most underappreciated shift is on the input side — who can use the software, not just what it produces

slides.headline: The AI Adoption Matrix
slides.subheadline: Where should humans stay in the loop?

quadrants.label: No Regrets
quadrants.desc: Explicit data + low error cost → AI does it all

quadrants.label: Quality Control
quadrants.desc: Explicit data + high error cost → AI produces, human verifies

quadrants.label: Creative Catalyst
quadrants.desc: Tacit knowledge + low error cost → AI creates options, human selects

quadrants.label: Human First
quadrants.desc: Tacit knowledge + high error cost → Human leads

slides.note: Adoption decisions hinge on two axes: type of information needed and cost of error

slides.headline: The More Important Strategic Impact
slides.subheadline: It's not about quality — it's about cost
bullets: Most debate focuses on AI improving offering quality
The bigger strategic impact is on a firm's cost structure
Even imperfect AI is worth adopting when error costs are low
Abundant explicit data + low error cost = strong case for full AI agents
Ryanair beat BA not by being better — by being cheaper at the same value
slides.note: Strategists must look past quality debates to where generative AI enables structural cost advantage

slides.headline: Industry-Level Disruption
slides.subheadline: Wide AI availability destroys margins — for incumbents
bullets: Generic AI adoption lowers barriers to entry across sectors
New competitors can match incumbents at a fraction of the cost
Blockbuster saw Netflix coming — and still failed to act decisively
Winning requires self-disruption before competitors do it for you
Broad AI use redistributes profits; leaders must take calculated risks
slides.note: The Blockbuster cautionary tale: awareness without action is not a strategy

slides.headline: Strategic Transformation: Two Imperatives
slides.subheadline: Winners rethink the whole organisation — not just the tools
bullets: 1. Reevaluate ALL assets as data — not just digital systems
2. Redesign the organisation from first principles around AI
Effective AI strategy requires acting now, not waiting for maturity
Customise models, integrate APIs, or purchase applications — choose deliberately
Long-term profitability requires creating more value with AI than competitors
slides.note: Two key questions: Where is the data in my organisation? What does my organisation need to look like?

slides.headline: Ethics & AI
slides.subheadline: The principles that must guide development and use

slides.headline: What Is AI Ethics?
slides.subheadline: The moral principles guiding AI development and use
bullets: AI systems must be designed to benefit society, not just shareholders
Core pillars: Fairness, Transparency, Privacy, Safety, Accountability
AI learns from data — biased data encodes and amplifies injustice
High-stakes decisions (medical, legal, financial) now delegated to models
Ethics ensures AI development is guided in a socially responsible direction
slides.note: Ethics is not a constraint on innovation — it is a prerequisite for trust

slides.headline: Seven Ethical Principles for AI Development

left.title: Governance Pillars
bullets: Human Agency & Oversight — humans own final decisions
Technical Robustness & Safety — no harm from errors
Privacy & Data Governance — strict rules on personal data
Transparency — explain decisions, steps, and data used

right.title: Societal Pillars
bullets: Diversity, Non-Discrimination & Fairness — AI works for everyone
Societal & Environmental Wellbeing — positive net impact
Accountability — clear ownership when things go wrong

slides.note: Drawn from EU AI Ethics Guidelines and the ADAPT Centre AI in My Life framework

slides.headline: Real-World Ethical Stakes
bullets: Judge used ChatGPT to advise on a child's medical rights — ruled unethical
Lawyer submitted AI-hallucinated case citations — faced court sanctions
AI-generated music floods the internet — artists call for urgent regulation
China's Social Credit System: 1,000 points, AI-monitored, reward/punish behaviour
Data centres consumed 22% of Ireland's energy in 2024 — growing with AI use
ChatGPT uses ~0.5 litres of water per 5–50 prompts for server cooling
slides.note: Ethics is not abstract — it shapes legal risk, social trust, and environmental impact today

slides.headline: AI and the Future of Work
slides.subheadline: Automation reaches white-collar professions — not just factories
bullets: Automation first threatened manual labour — now targets translation, law, medicine
In-house legal teams grew 200%+ (1997–2019) as firms reduced external law firms
AI lowers the variable cost of cognitive labour toward zero
New competitors can now enter knowledge industries with minimal human capital
The question is not if AI replaces roles — it's which roles, and when
slides.note: Entire professional categories may not disappear — but the economics of those professions will transform

slides.headline: Ethical AI in Practice: The Adoption Test
slides.subheadline: Apply the matrix to human-AI decisions, not just business tasks
bullets: Low stakes + explicit data → AI can act autonomously (email routing, scheduling)
High stakes + tacit knowledge → Human must lead (expelling a student, hiring)
ChatGPT in homework: AI assistance ≠ academic dishonesty — context matters
Trust between educators and students requires new transparency norms
Citizens must engage in AI governance — not just accept it passively
slides.note: The Adoption Matrix is not just a business tool — it is an ethical decision framework

slides.headline: Key Takeaways
slides.subheadline: What every leader and citizen must internalise
bullets: Gen AI will keep improving — anticipate it, don't wait for certainty
AI fundamentally changes who can access and use computing power
Even weak AI may be worth adopting — cost savings can override quality gaps
Broad AI raises competitive intensity; self-disruption is the survival move
Ethics is not optional — bias, privacy, and accountability demand proactive governance
Two essential questions: Where is our data? What must our organisation become?
slides.note: Strategy and ethics are not in tension — both require anticipation, intentionality, and courage to act

slides.headline: The Age of AI Demands Both Strategy and Ethics
slides.subheadline: The firms and societies that thrive will be those that act with both intelligence and integrity.
slides.note: Contact for further discussion on AI strategy, ethics, and organisational transformation
